name: Allie Reynolds
hof: Not inducted. Peak 33.6% on BBWAA ballot (1968). Monument Park plaque (1989). 6× WS Champ.

stat_justification.stf: 2.06 ERA in 1952 — led the AL. ERA+ of 161. K/9 of 5.89 is tantalizingly close to the 6.0 bonus threshold but misses. Reynolds had a blazing fastball — coach Hank Iba discovered him throwing javelins and saw the arm speed. Birdie Tebbetts compared him to the fastest pitchers of the era. But his dominance came from a complete repertoire (fastball, curve, slider) rather than overwhelming pure stuff. Rating of 3.
stat_justification.ctl: BB/9 of 3.57 in 1952 — 97 walks in 244 innings. Reynolds was wild his entire career. He led the majors in HBP in 1943 and in walks in 1945 (130). Even in his best years, he walked too many batters. He relied on stuff and guts rather than precision. WHIP 1.12. Rating of 0 — his control was genuinely poor.
stat_justification.sta: 244.1 IP in 1952 with 24 complete games. But Reynolds was unique: he was both a starter and a reliever throughout his career. 309 starts and 125 relief appearances. 40 career saves alongside 182 wins. His IP was lower than pure aces because the Yankees used him flexibly. Rating of 2.
stat_justification.def: No standout defensive metrics as a pitcher. Reynolds was an adequate fielder. He did bat .308 in World Series play — unusually good for a pitcher. But no Gold Glove equivalence. Rating of 0.
stat_justification.clu: World Series: 7-2, 2.79 ERA in 77 innings across 15 games. Won or saved the clinching game in 1950 (10 IP, 1 ER in Game 2), 1952 (save in Game 6, win in Game 7), and 1953 (save in Game 6). 1949 WS Game 1: 2-hit shutout, 1-0. Two no-hitters in 1951, including a pennant-clincher. The man was made for October. Maximum clutch rating of 3.

personality.leadership_style: Quiet authority rooted in physical dominance. Reynolds wasn't a rah-rah leader — he was the man teammates trusted with the ball when everything was on the line. Bobby Brown called him 'the one at the top, the real leader.' Manager Casey Stengel used him in every possible role because Reynolds never refused. He led by answering the call, not by making speeches.
personality.temperament: Stoic, unflappable, ice-cold under pressure. When Yogi Berra dropped the foul pop that would have completed his second no-hitter, Reynolds walked over and said: 'Don't worry Yogi, we'll get him again.' He then threw the exact same pitch to Ted Williams, who popped up again. Berra caught it this time. Reynolds played with bone chips in his elbow in 1951 and never mentioned it. Muscogee warrior ethos — endurance without complaint.
personality.work_ethic: Relentless versatility. Reynolds started, relieved, closed — whatever the team needed. He threw 244 IP in 1952 while also recording 6 saves. He pitched in 15 World Series games — 9 starts, 6 relief appearances. His willingness to do everything asked of him is the defining trait. He also built an oil business during his playing years, arriving at spring training as both a pitcher and a businessman.
personality.lifestyle: Oklahoma through and through. Born in Bethany, attended Oklahoma A&M (now Oklahoma State), returned to Oklahoma after retirement. Member of the Muscogee (Creek) Nation — his heritage was central to his identity, though he was uncomfortable with the 'Chief' nickname because of the sacred meaning of that title. Built a successful oil business while still playing. Married to Earlene, raised a family. Became president of the revived American Association after baseball.
personality.era_adaptability: HIGH. Reynolds's fastball-curve-slider combination, combined with his willingness to start or relieve, would make him a modern manager's dream. He'd be the ideal 'opener' or 'fireman' in today's game — or a dominant starter if used traditionally. His walks would be a concern, but his October pedigree is timeless. The two no-hitters in one season places him in elite company regardless of era.
personality.clubhouse_impact: RESPECTED-STEADY. Reynolds was part of the legendary 'Big Three' with Vic Raschi and Eddie Lopat. The three starters anchored five consecutive World Series championships (1949-53). Reynolds was the power arm, Raschi the workhorse, Lopat the craftsman. Teammates called him 'Chief' behind his back — with deep respect, not mockery. Bobby Brown: 'We felt he was the one at the top.'
personality.dark_side: The Hall of Fame snub. Reynolds peaked at 33.6% on the BBWAA ballot in 1968 — ahead of future HOFers Newhouser, Rizzuto, Reese, and Kell — but never got closer. His versatility (starter/reliever split) hurt his counting stats. His career was shortened by bone chips and a bus accident in 1953 that aggravated a back injury. He was considered by the Veterans Committee in 2009 and 2014 but not selected. In ILB terms: Reynolds carries a 'Hall of Fame Snub' trait — his legacy is forever incomplete despite extraordinary accomplishment.

chemistry_traits.desc: Muscogee (Creek) Nation member. +1 respect from all teammates. The name evokes 'elegance, power, and speed' — like the Santa Fe railroad train.
7-2 WS record, 2.79 ERA. In elimination games and clinching scenarios, +2 STF. Reynolds was built for pressure.
Reynolds-Raschi-Lopat anchored 5 straight WS titles. When all three are on the same staff, +2 team pitching rating.
Can start or relieve in any game with no penalty. 309 starts, 125 relief appearances, 40 saves. Ultimate versatility.
Joe DiMaggio personally recommended the Yankees trade for Reynolds. +1 chemistry with DiMaggio and all Yankee legends.
Played the 1951 season with bone chips in his throwing elbow. 10% chance per season of injury flaring up, causing -1 STF.
Built an oil business while still playing. +1 to post-career financial stability. Reynolds never needed baseball after baseball.
When Berra dropped the no-hitter foul pop: 'We'll get him again.' Prevents teammate panic after errors — +1 composure to catcher.

preferred_locations.note: Start, relieve, close — Reynolds lived on the mound in every role.
preferred_locations.note_extra: Source labeled 'Pitcher's Mound' / affinity 'HIGH' — mapped to Practice Field/HIGH as the pitching-craft domain. Eighth Practice Field/HIGH in the set. Reynolds joins the mound sub-domain pitchers (Mathewson, McGinnity, Phillippe, Plank, Reulbach) plus the batting-cages (Puckett), infield-fielding (Reese), and hitter-study (Reulbach) sub-domains. Reynolds's is the multi-role mound HIGH: 'Start, relieve, close.' Where prior mound HIGHs encoded a specific pitching identity (Mathewson's mound-as-cerebral-stage, McGinnity's iron-man volume, Plank's 17-year office, Reulbach's hit-suppression domain), Reynolds's encodes positional fluidity — the mound as universal deployment zone. The chemistry_trait 'Swiss Army Arm' (no penalty for any role) is the mechanical expression.

preferred_locations.note: Quiet presence, respected leader. The man they wanted on the mound when it mattered.
preferred_locations.note_extra: Source labeled 'Clubhouse / Locker Room' / affinity 'HIGH' — mapped to Hotel/Rest/HIGH. Tenth Hotel/Rest/HIGH register in the set: vice-social (McGraw), pastoral care (McKechnie), joy-distribution (Musial), predatory preparation (Newhouser), proprietary home-base (O'Doul), communal-emotional-center (Pesky), kinetic-social-preparation (Reiser), pregame-domain-ownership (Puckett), moral-authority-center (Reese), and now quiet-authority-trust (Reynolds). Reynolds's is the most minimally active Hotel/Rest/HIGH in the set: 'Quiet presence, respected leader.' Where Puckett's 30-minute pre-game domain was kinetic and verbal, Reynolds's clubhouse presence is defined by what he doesn't need to do — he doesn't need to be loud because the ball in his hand on October afternoons has already spoken. The personality.leadership_style note ('He led by answering the call, not by making speeches') confirms.

preferred_locations.note: Born and buried in Oklahoma. Creek Nation heritage. Oil business. Always went home.
preferred_locations.note_extra: Source labeled 'Oklahoma / Home' / affinity 'HIGH' — mapped to Community Events/HIGH as the hometown-origin domain. Ninth hometown/origin Community Events/HIGH in the set. Reynolds's is the most identity-layered: 'Born and buried in Oklahoma' (full lifecycle arc), 'Creek Nation heritage' (tribal homeland), and 'Oil business' (commercial homeland). Three distinct Oklahoma identity threads converge in a single HIGH. Prior hometown HIGHs encoded one primary thread (Plank/Gettysburg = battlefield honor, Reese/Louisville = born-raised-buried + Louisville Slugger connection). Reynolds's Oklahoma HIGH is the only one that explicitly names Indigenous homeland identity as part of the geographic affinity. The chemistry_trait 'Superchief' and action card 'The Muscogee Nation's Pitcher' expand the tribal-homeland dimension.

preferred_locations.note: Built oil business during playing career. Arrived at spring training as businessman-pitcher.
preferred_locations.note_extra: Source labeled 'Oil Field / Business Office' / affinity 'MEDIUM' — first commercial/business-enterprise preferred_location in the set. No prior card has an oil field, business office, or non-baseball commercial enterprise as a named domain. Mapped to Community Events/MEDIUM as a professional-outside-baseball domain. The personality.work_ethic note ('He also built an oil business during his playing years, arriving at spring training as both a pitcher and a businessman') and personality.lifestyle ('Built a successful oil business while still playing') and chemistry_trait 'Oil Baron' (+1 post-career financial stability) all confirm. Reynolds is the first card in the set where a second non-baseball career is a named preferred_location domain during the playing years rather than as a post-career transition. This distinguishes it from Reese's Broadcasting Booth MEDIUM (post-career) and Ott's Broadcast Booth MEDIUM (post-career) — Reynolds's business MEDIUM was concurrent, not sequential.

preferred_locations.note: Social within the team. Part of the Big Three camaraderie with Raschi and Lopat.
preferred_locations.note_extra: Source labeled 'Restaurant / Team Dinner' / affinity 'MEDIUM' — mapped to Hotel/Rest/MEDIUM as the team-social-dining domain. Fifth Hotel/Rest/MEDIUM in the set. The Big Three camaraderie (Reynolds-Raschi-Lopat) is the specific social basis of this MEDIUM — dinners as staff bonding rather than civic presence (O'Doul's restaurant) or integration diplomacy (Reese's Bar/Social). The chemistry_trait 'Big Three' (+2 team pitching rating when all three on same staff) is the cross-field expression of the dining-camaraderie basis.

preferred_locations.note: Not a nightlife player. Professional, focused.
preferred_locations.note_extra: Source labeled 'Bar / Nightlife' / affinity 'LOW' — first Bar/Nightlife LOW in the set. Prior Bar/Nightlife entries: Puckett (MEDIUM with Hidden Edge trigger coupling), Reese (MEDIUM as diplomatic sociability). Reynolds's LOW completes the Bar/Nightlife affinity spectrum: LOW (Reynolds — professional abstinence), MEDIUM (Puckett — extroversion with risk; Reese — social glue). The LOW is the most simply stated in the set: 'Not a nightlife player. Professional, focused.' No elaboration, no moral complexity, no trait trigger — pure career-priority abstinence. Thirteenth Hotel/Rest/LOW register overall: the simplest single-reason LOW in the domain taxonomy.

Source labeled 'Gambling Hall' / affinity 'NONE' — seventh NONE in the set. Seventh Gambling Hall/NONE. The pattern continues: working-class/heritage-background players across eras (Pesky, Reiser, Puckett, Reese, Reynolds) plus the principled exclusion (Newhouser) and simple biographical void (Mungo). Reynolds's void adds a Native American / Oklahoma oil-business player to the working-class Gambling Hall/NONE cohort.

hot_triggers: World Series and elimination games — Reynolds elevated his game in October every time.
Pennant race pressure — second no-hitter was a pennant-clincher vs. Boston.
Ace-vs-ace matchups — thrived when facing Feller, Lemon, and other top starters.
Dual-role deployment — when asked to start AND relieve in a series, Reynolds gets better.
cold_triggers: Control lapses — Reynolds could lose the strike zone for stretches, walking 4+ in a game.
Physical ailments — bone chips, back injury from 1953 bus accident limited late-career effectiveness.
Early-season rust — Reynolds often started slowly before heating up in summer and fall.
momentum.pressure_response: ELITE OCTOBER PERFORMER. Reynolds's World Series numbers (7-2, 2.79 ERA, 77 IP) place him among the greatest postseason pitchers ever. He won or saved the clinching game in three different World Series. He threw a 2-hit shutout in Game 1 of the 1949 WS, then came back to save Game 4. In 1952, he won Game 4, saved Game 6, and won Game 7 in relief. The pattern is unmistakable: the bigger the moment, the better Reynolds pitched. Maximum pressure performance.

action_card_seeds.title: DiMaggio's Recommendation
action_card_seeds.text: Your team needs a pitcher. The opposing GM offers you a choice of two arms. Your franchise player whispers: 'Take Reynolds.' You listen. He becomes the ace of a dynasty.
action_card_seeds.origin: Cleveland offered the Yankees any pitcher except Feller for Joe Gordon. DiMaggio told GM Larry MacPhail to take Reynolds over Red Embree. DiMaggio was right — Reynolds anchored 6 WS titles.

action_card_seeds.title: Two No-Hitters in One Season
action_card_seeds.text: Your ace throws his second no-hitter of the season. On the last out, the catcher drops the pop foul. Your pitcher walks over calmly and says: 'Don't worry — we'll get him again.' Same pitch. Same pop-up. Caught this time. The pennant is clinched.
action_card_seeds.origin: September 28, 1951. Reynolds's second no-hitter — a pennant-clincher vs. Boston. Ted Williams popped up. Berra dropped it. Reynolds threw the same pitch. Williams popped up again. Berra caught it.

action_card_seeds.title: The Clincher
action_card_seeds.text: Your ace starts the series. Then he comes out of the bullpen to save the next game. Then he relieves again to win the clincher. One pitcher, three appearances, one championship.
action_card_seeds.origin: 1952 WS: Reynolds started Game 1 (loss), started and won Game 4 (2-0 shutout), saved Game 6 (2 outs), won Game 7 in relief (3 IP). Three appearances in four games to close the series.

action_card_seeds.title: The Javelin Thrower
action_card_seeds.text: A college coach spots a track athlete throwing javelins and sees a pitcher's arm. He recruits the athlete to the baseball team. The javelin thrower becomes a multi-sport star, then a professional baseball ace.
action_card_seeds.origin: Oklahoma A&M coach Hank Iba spotted Reynolds throwing javelins in 1937. He recruited him to baseball. Reynolds became a two-sport star before choosing pro baseball over the NFL.

action_card_seeds.title: The Oil Well Investor
action_card_seeds.text: Your star pitcher spends winters building an oil business. By the time he retires from baseball, he's already wealthy. He never needs the game again — he walks away on his own terms.
action_card_seeds.origin: Reynolds invested in Oklahoma oil wells during his playing career. After retirement, the oil business made him financially independent — rare for a player of his era.

action_card_seeds.title: Swiss Army Pitcher
action_card_seeds.text: Your manager needs a starter on Tuesday, a reliever on Thursday, and a closer on Saturday. One pitcher volunteers for all three. He goes 7-2 in the postseason doing everything asked of him.
action_card_seeds.origin: Reynolds made 309 starts and 125 relief appearances. He had 182 wins and 40 saves. In the WS, he started 9 games and relieved in 6, recording wins or saves in each relief outing.

action_card_seeds.title: Playing Through Bone Chips
action_card_seeds.text: Your ace has bone chips in his throwing elbow. He tells nobody. He throws two no-hitters, finishes 3rd in MVP voting, and helps the team win the World Series. The injury is revealed only afterward.
action_card_seeds.origin: Reynolds pitched the entire 1951 season — including both no-hitters and the World Series — with bone chips in his right elbow. He never disclosed the injury during the season.

action_card_seeds.title: The Muscogee Nation's Pitcher
action_card_seeds.text: A Native American pitcher becomes one of the greatest in his league. The press calls him 'Chief' and 'Superchief.' He's uncomfortable with the title — in his culture, 'chief' is sacred. But his teammates use it with genuine reverence, not mockery. A statue is erected on the reservation.
action_card_seeds.origin: Reynolds, a Muscogee (Creek) Nation member, was nicknamed 'Superchief' after the Santa Fe Railroad train. Bobby Brown said the name fit for 'elegance, power, and speed.' The Muscogee Nation erected a statue in his honor.

art_direction.face: Ruggedly handsome at 6'0" 195 lbs. Strong, athletic build. Native American features — high cheekbones, dark hair, intense dark eyes. The bearing of quiet authority — not flashy, not boastful, just utterly confident. The face of a man who threw two no-hitters in one season with bone chips in his elbow.
art_direction.attire: New York Yankees home pinstripes, 1952 style. Classic postwar wool flannel. Right-handed pitcher in mid-delivery — the blazing fastball about to leave his hand. Cap pulled low, shadowing the eyes. The embodiment of Yankee pitching dominance.
art_direction.mood: Ice-cold composure. The moment after Berra dropped the ball — Reynolds walking over, calm as stone, saying 'We'll get him again.' No panic, no frustration, just absolute certainty. The card should feel inevitable, like the outcome was never in doubt.
art_direction.style: Sepia-toned with warm golden highlights, unified ILB portrait style. Yankee Stadium in the early 1950s — the cathedral of baseball, packed for a World Series game. October light filtering through the upper deck. The card should feel like dynasty — five straight championships are being forged.
art_direction.reference: Think the Yankees at their most dominant — the Big Three era, DiMaggio fading into Mantle's dawn. Reynolds was the bridge between dynasties, the constant. The card should convey both power and reliability — this is the man you give the ball to when everything is on the line.
